era_context: Late Bronze Age, as above. The year immediately preceding -1520, during the reigns of Thutmose IV (Egypt), the Mitanni kingdom (Syria/Mesopotamia), and the Shang Dynasty (China). This was a period of relative stability in the Eastern Mediterranean, with palace-based economies at their height.

events.title: Reign of Thutmose IV continues (Egypt)
events.description: Thutmose IV, the ninth pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty, continued his reign over Egypt. His rule (c. 1400–1390 BCE in conventional chronology) was marked by diplomatic marriages and treaties with foreign powers, particularly the Mitanni kingdom. He commissioned the restoration of the Great Sphinx of Giza and maintained Egyptian military and economic dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean. His reign was relatively peaceful compared to his predecessors, emphasising diplomacy over military conquest.
events.certainty_note: Thutmose IV's regnal dates are established within ±2–3 years by Egyptological consensus, though absolute chronology remains debated. The assignment to -1521 is approximate.

events.title: Mitanni kingdom at height of power
events.description: The Mitanni kingdom, a major power in northern Syria and Mesopotamia, was at or near its height of influence during this period. The Mitanni, an Indo-European people who had established a chariot-based military aristocracy, controlled trade routes and maintained diplomatic relations with Egypt, the Hittites, and Babylon. This period saw the reign of kings such as Shuttarna II or Artatama II, whose names appear in Egyptian diplomatic correspondence.
key_figures: Mitanni rulers (Shuttarna II or Artatama II, uncertain for this specific year)
events.certainty_note: Mitanni chronology is uncertain; the kingdom's peak is generally placed c. 1500–1350 BCE, but precise year-by-year dating is not possible. Assignment to -1521 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Thutmose IV's reign (c. 1400–1390 BCE) is debated by scholars advocating for alternative chronologies. Some argue for dates 10–20 years earlier or later. The Mitanni kingdom's chronology is even more uncertain, with some scholars placing its peak earlier (c. 1550 BCE) and others later (c. 1400 BCE).
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1521 BCE is dominated by Egyptian and Near Eastern sources. The Amarna Letters, though written later (c. 1350 BCE), provide retrospective evidence of diplomatic relations during this period. The record is heavily skewed toward elite political and military events. Economic, social, and demographic changes are largely invisible. Absolute chronology remains uncertain by ±10–50 years.

graph_edges.note: Thutmose IV's reign ended with the accession of Amenhotep III
